title: Closed bolt

infobox.value: This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Closed_bolt "Special:EditPage/Closed bolt") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(December 2016)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))*

or **closed breech** is one where, when ready to fire, a round is in the [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(weaponry) "Chamber (weaponry)") and the [bolt](/wiki/Bolt_(firearm) "Bolt (firearm)") and working parts are forward *in battery*. When the trigger is pulled, the [firing pin](/wiki/Firing_pin "Firing pin") or striker fires the round; the action is cycled by the energy of the shot, sending the bolt to the rear, which extracts and ejects the empty [cartridge case](/wiki/Cartridge_case "Cartridge case"); and the bolt goes forward, feeding a fresh round from the [magazine](/wiki/Magazine_(firearm) "Magazine (firearm)") into the chamber, ready for the next shot.

Woodman, Harry (1997). *Spandau Guns, Windsock Mini-Datafile No.10*. Albatros Publications Ltd. pp. 2–3. [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [0-948414-90-1](/wiki/Special:BookSources/0-948414-90-1 "Special:BookSources/0-948414-90-1").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

System in firearms

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Gewehr_G36_noBG.jpg/500px-Gewehr_G36_noBG.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gewehr_G36_noBG.jpg)

A [Heckler & Koch G36](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_G36 "Heckler & Koch G36"), an example of a rifle that fires from a closed bolt

A [semi](/wiki/Semi-automatic_firearm "Semi-automatic firearm") or [fully automatic](/wiki/Fully_automatic "Fully automatic") [firearm](/wiki/Firearm "Firearm") which is said to fire from a **closed bolt** or **closed breech** is one where, when ready to fire, a round is in the [chamber](/wiki/Chamber_(weaponry) "Chamber (weaponry)") and the [bolt](/wiki/Bolt_(firearm) "Bolt (firearm)") and working parts are forward *in battery*. When the trigger is pulled, the [firing pin](/wiki/Firing_pin "Firing pin") or striker fires the round; the action is cycled by the energy of the shot, sending the bolt to the rear, which extracts and ejects the empty [cartridge case](/wiki/Cartridge_case "Cartridge case"); and the bolt goes forward, feeding a fresh round from the [magazine](/wiki/Magazine_(firearm) "Magazine (firearm)") into the chamber, ready for the next shot.

## World War I aircraft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Closed_bolt&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: World War I aircraft")]

When [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") era machine guns were being tried for use on aircraft, the [Lewis gun](/wiki/Lewis_gun "Lewis gun") was found not to be usable with a [gun synchronizer](/wiki/Synchronization_gear#At_the_gun "Synchronization gear") for forward firing through the propeller, due to its firing cycle starting with an [open bolt](/wiki/Open_bolt "Open bolt"). Maxim style arms fired with a cycle starting with a closed bolt, and since the bullet firing from the gun started the firing cycle, it was much easier to set the synchronizer to trigger the gun only when the propeller's blade was not directly in front of the gun's muzzle. These included:

* [Vickers machine gun](/wiki/Vickers_machine_gun "Vickers machine gun")
* Both the rectangular-receiver [lMG 08](/wiki/MG_08#Aircraft_versions "MG 08")[[1]](#cite_note-1) and lightened-receiver LMG 08/15 [Spandau gun](/wiki/Maschinengewehr_08 "Maschinengewehr 08"), and [Parabellum LMG 14 gun](/wiki/Parabellum_MG14 "Parabellum MG14")
* Improvements introduced by Swedish armaments designer Carl Gustave Swebilius to the American [M1895 Colt–Browning machine gun](/wiki/M1895_Colt%E2%80%93Browning_machine_gun "M1895 Colt–Browning machine gun") for aircraft use, creating the [M1917 and M1918 Marlin-Rockwell machine guns](/wiki/M1895_Colt%E2%80%93Browning_machine_gun#World_War_I "M1895 Colt–Browning machine gun") for the [USAAS](/wiki/United_States_Army_Air_Service "United States Army Air Service")

## Comparison with open bolt design

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Closed_bolt&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Comparison with open bolt design")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Closed_bolt "Special:EditPage/Closed bolt") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(December 2016)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

A closed bolt design has both advantages and disadvantages when compared to an [open bolt](/wiki/Open_bolt "Open bolt") design:

### Advantages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Closed_bolt&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Advantages")]

* More accurate for the first round and for semi-automatic fire:
  + No movement of heavy working parts prior to firing to potentially inhibit accuracy.
  + Round sits consistently in the chamber.
  + Potentially shorter delay between operator pulling the trigger and round being fired (also known as lock time).
* Cleaner operation – less potential for dust and other foreign debris to enter the gun, since the action remains closed unless the weapon is firing.
* Action can be locked forward to further reduce noise in a [suppressed](/wiki/Suppressor "Suppressor") weapon.
* Can carry an additional round in the chamber, increasing ammunition capacity by one round beyond the [magazine](/wiki/Magazine_(firearm) "Magazine (firearm)")'s maximum limit.

### Disadvantages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Closed_bolt&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Disadvantages")]

* More complicated and expensive to manufacture.
* Less heat [dissipation](/wiki/Dissipation "Dissipation") from closed chamber (increased danger of [cooking off](/wiki/Cooking_off "Cooking off"))
* The extra round in the chamber can be a safety hazard, as even when the magazine is removed, the round in the chamber will still be ready to fire, and must be manually ejected.

### Uses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Closed_bolt&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Uses")]

Closed-bolt designs are often used in [rifles](/wiki/Rifle "Rifle"). The improved accuracy of closed-bolt weapons is more desirable, while the poorer heat dissipation is less of an issue for slower-firing weapons. In contrast, open-bolt designs are more often used in automatic weapons, such as [machine guns](/wiki/Machine_gun "Machine gun"). For fast-firing automatic weapons, heat will rapidly build up from sustained firing, but accuracy is of less importance. Thus, the improved heat dissipation of open-bolt designs is generally more desirable in automatic weapons.

## Closed-bolt firearms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Closed_bolt&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Closed-bolt firearms")]

Examples of closed-bolt firearms include:

* [AR-15 style rifle](/wiki/AR-15_style_rifle "AR-15 style rifle")
* [Armalite AR-10](/wiki/Armalite_AR-10 "Armalite AR-10")
* [Armalite AR-16](/wiki/Armalite_AR-16 "Armalite AR-16")
* [Armalite AR-18](/wiki/Armalite_AR-18 "Armalite AR-18")
* [FAMAE SAF](/wiki/FAMAE_SAF "FAMAE SAF")
* [FN F2000](/wiki/FN_F2000 "FN F2000")
* [Heckler & Koch MP5](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_MP5 "Heckler & Koch MP5")
* [Heckler & Koch UMP](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_UMP "Heckler & Koch UMP")
* [Heckler & Koch G3](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_G3 "Heckler & Koch G3")
* [Heckler & Koch G36](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_G36 "Heckler & Koch G36")
* [Heckler & Koch HK21](/wiki/Heckler_%26_Koch_HK21 "Heckler & Koch HK21")
* [Kalashnikov rifle](/wiki/Kalashnikov_rifle "Kalashnikov rifle")
* [Maxim gun](/wiki/Maxim_gun "Maxim gun")
* [Mendoza HM-3](/wiki/Mendoza_HM-3 "Mendoza HM-3")
* [MG 08](/wiki/MG_08 "MG 08")
* [MG 13](/wiki/MG_13 "MG 13")
* [MG 17 machine gun](/wiki/MG_17_machine_gun "MG 17 machine gun")
* [MG 131 machine gun](/wiki/MG_131_machine_gun "MG 131 machine gun")
* [M2 Browning machine gun](/wiki/M2_Browning_machine_gun "M2 Browning machine gun")
* [M16 rifle](/wiki/M16_rifle "M16 rifle")
* [M1895 Colt–Browning machine gun](/wiki/M1895_Colt%E2%80%93Browning_machine_gun "M1895 Colt–Browning machine gun")
* [Parabellum MG14](/wiki/Parabellum_MG14 "Parabellum MG14")
* [SIG 550](/wiki/SIG_550 "SIG 550")
* [Spectre M4](/wiki/Spectre_M4 "Spectre M4")
* [Steyr AUG](/wiki/Steyr_AUG "Steyr AUG")
* [Vickers Machine Gun](/wiki/Vickers_Machine_Gun "Vickers Machine Gun")

## Mixed mode firearms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Closed_bolt&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Mixed mode firearms")]

Examples of mixed mode firearms (capable of operating from either an open bolt or closed bolt) include:

* [ArmaLite AR-100](/wiki/ArmaLite_AR-100 "ArmaLite AR-100") - AR-101 and AR-102
* [CETME Model A](/wiki/CETME_rifle#Model_A "CETME rifle")
* [FG 42](/wiki/FG_42 "FG 42")
* [FN SCAR](/wiki/FN_SCAR "FN SCAR") - Heat Adaptive Modular Rifle
* [LWRC Infantry Automatic Rifle](/wiki/LWRC_Infantry_Automatic_Rifle "LWRC Infantry Automatic Rifle") - M6A4
* [M1941 Johnson machine gun](/wiki/M1941_Johnson_machine_gun "M1941 Johnson machine gun")
* [Dror light machine gun](/wiki/Dror_light_machine_gun "Dror light machine gun")
